Renaissance Early Modern

Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Early Modern

Thomas Hobbes

1588 – 1679

A thoroughgoing materialist: thought as motion in the body, the mind as mechanism.

The governing thesis is this: Hobbes replaces the scholastic and Cartesian picture of an immaterial “soul” with a mechanistic physiology of sense, imagination, memory, appetite, will, speech, and reasoning. But he does not merely discard God, angels, spirit, inspiration, or resurrection as “theological distractions.” He treats them as decisive boundary cases for any philosophy of mind: they test what can be conceived, what can be named, what counts as a substance, how Scripture should discipline metaphysical language, and whether a mind must be located in a body. In Hobbes, finite human mentality is bodily motion in an organized living creature; divine and angelic mentality must be approached through a stricter semantics of body, place, spirit, revelation, and honour.

Hobbes’s philosophy of mind is therefore best described as a materialist, empiricist, nominalist, conative, and mortalist theory of minded life, with a serious theological perimeter. It is materialist because all natural mental acts are motions in bodies. It is empiricist because “all conception in the mind” originates in sense. It is nominalist because universals, definitions, truth, falsity, and science depend on names. It is conative because appetite, aversion, endeavour, deliberation, and will are small beginnings and sequences of bodily motion. It is mortalist because Hobbes denies that Scripture or natural philosophy proves a naturally immortal, separable, incorporeal soul. Yet it is not a simple atheistic reduction: Hobbes’s God remains the ultimate sovereign and cause; his account of angels is not simply disbelief; and his later writings move toward an explicit doctrine of divine corporeity.

1. Corpus and methodological orientation

Hobbes’s philosophy of mind is spread across several works rather than contained in a single treatise. The early Elements of Law / Human Nature opens with “the faculties, acts, and passions, of the soul of man,” and immediately treats sense, imagination, memory, discourse, language, appetite, aversion, pleasure, pain, and will as the foundation for politics. Hobbes begins with human psychology because political science, for him, depends on a correct account of what human beings are.

The mature system is distributed across Leviathan, De Corpore, De Homine, and De Cive. The Stanford Encyclopedia notes that The Elements of Law begins with mind and language before politics, while the later projected philosophical system is divided into body, man, and citizen. It also stresses that Hobbes’s psychology is rooted in physiology and physics, though the unity of his whole system remains debated.

For the present purpose, the key texts are these: Human Nature / The Elements of Law for the early psychology of faculties and passions; Leviathan I–VI for sense, imagination, speech, reason, passion, deliberation, and will; Leviathan XXXIV, XXXVIII, XLIV, and XLVI for spirit, angels, soul, resurrection, incorporeal substance, and the critique of scholastic metaphysics; De Corpore XXV for the mature physiological theory of sense and animal motion; and the Latin Leviathan Appendix and late anti-Bramhall materials for Hobbes’s increasingly explicit language about a corporeal God. Contemporary scholarship in English, French, and Spanish-language venues is especially helpful on the last issue, because the relation between Hobbes’s materialism and his theology is still disputed.

2. Fundamental ontology: body, place, motion, and the rejection of incorporeal substance

The foundation of Hobbes’s philosophy of mind is not a theory of consciousness but an ontology. For Hobbes, what exists in the universe is body. A body is something that occupies or fills a portion of space; substance and body are not two kinds of being but, in strict philosophical usage, effectively the same category. In Leviathan XXXIV, he says that “body” signifies what fills or occupies room, and that “substance” and “body” signify the same thing; hence “substance incorporeal” is a self-destructive phrase.

The same claim appears in the more general metaphysical chapters. In Leviathan XLVI, Hobbes says the universe is corporeal, every part of it is body, and what is not body is no part of the universe — effectively nowhere and nothing. He allows that “incorporeal” may sometimes be used as a name of honour for God, but not as a literal positive description of a kind of substance.

This matters for mind because Hobbes thinks the immaterial soul is usually produced by bad grammar, confused abstraction, or scholastic metaphysics. If “soul” is taken as a substance, then, on Hobbes’s strict ontology, it must either be a body or it is not a real substance at all. If it is said to be “incorporeal substance,” the phrase combines mutually cancelling terms. French scholarship on Hobbes and Port-Royal captures this point well: Hobbes’s attack on “spirit” as “incorporeal substance” is part of a broader critique of definitions that do not signify anything coherent, and it directly undermines the scholastic doctrine of the naturally immortal separated soul.

This is also where Hobbes differs sharply from Descartes and from Henry More. In his anti-Cartesian line of argument, Hobbes denies that the cogito licenses an immaterial thinking substance. A subject of thought must be located; but for Hobbes, to be located is to have bodily extension. Henry More later accepts the “located” character of spirit but argues for spiritual extension; Hobbes rejects that move, insisting that extension belongs to body.

A useful reconstruction is therefore:

The Hobbesian mind is not an immaterial container of ideas. It is the activity of an organized living body under the causal pressure of the world, the residual motions of sense, the appetitive and aversive motions around vital life, and the linguistic operations by which human beings compute consequences.

3. Life and mind as motion

Hobbes’s famous opening analogy in Leviathan already gives the philosophical frame. Life is “but a motion of limbs”; the heart is like a spring, the nerves like strings, and the joints like wheels. The commonwealth is an “artificial man,” whose sovereignty is an artificial soul, whose magistrates and officers are artificial joints, whose counsellors are artificial memory, and whose laws are artificial reason and will.

This analogy is not decorative. It announces a mechanistic program. Human beings are living automata, but “automaton” does not mean mindless in Hobbes’s sense. It means that minded activity itself is to be explained through motions internal to a living body. Human nature is not divided into an animal body plus an immaterial rational soul; rather, the cognitive and motive faculties are functions of a living organism. In Human Nature, Hobbes divides the powers of man into nutrition, motion, generation, sense, and reason; then he subdivides mental power into cognitive and motive powers. Cognitive power consists in images, conceptions, ideas, and knowledge; motive power concerns appetite, aversion, and animal motion.

This does not make Hobbes a crude behaviorist. He is not saying that only outward behavior exists. Internal states are real: they are internal motions of the organs, nerves, brain, heart, blood, and “spirits” in the older physiological sense. Nor is he a panpsychist. De Corpore explicitly denies that all bodies have sense; sense belongs properly to living creatures with the relevant organs and internal motions.

The result is a mechanistic animal psychology: mental events are not independent of physiology, but neither are they eliminated. They are redescribed as bodily processes in a living system.

4. Sense: the origin of all finite thought

Hobbes’s theory of mind begins with sensation. In Leviathan I, he says that thoughts are appearances or representations of qualities and accidents of bodies outside us, and that “there is no conception in a man’s mind” that has not first arisen, wholly or partly, from sense.

The causal model is mechanical. An external object presses on the organ of sense. That pressure is continued inward through nerves and membranes toward the brain and heart. The body responds with a counter-pressure, resistance, or “endeavour” outward. The resulting appearance is sense. The qualities we perceive — colour, sound, odour, heat, and the like — are not little sensible forms emitted by objects; they are motions in us, “seemings” or “fancies” produced by the object’s motion. Hobbes sharply rejects the scholastic doctrine of visible, audible, or intelligible “species” travelling from object to mind.

De Corpore gives the more technical version. Sense is an internal motion generated by motion from the object through the medium to the organ; the outward reaction or endeavour of the organ produces the phantasm. Hobbes’s formal definition is that sense is a phantasm made by outward reaction in the organ, caused by inward endeavour from the object, and remaining for some time.

This theory has several important consequences.

First, Hobbes is a realist about external bodies but not about sensible qualities as they appear. The object exists and causally affects us; but colour, sound, taste, and similar sensible qualities are phantasms in the sentient creature rather than literal accidents existing in the object as they seem to us. Human Nature gives the example of a blow to the eye producing an apparition of light: what is physically real is motion in the optic nerve and internal organs, while the light is the apparition produced in the subject.

Second, Hobbes is not a simple resemblance theorist. Sensory appearances are caused by bodies but do not need to resemble them. In modern terms, Hobbes offers a causal theory of perception combined with an appearance theory of sensible qualities.

Third, sense is already embodied and outward-projecting. The phantasm appears outside because the body reacts outward to inward motion. This is a primitive account of intentional directedness: the mind seems to be directed toward an external object because the organism’s internal reaction is outwardly oriented.

Fourth, sense is not enough for science. It is the first principle of knowledge, but science requires names, definitions, and reckoning. De Corpore says physics begins from appearances, and sense is the principle of knowledge, but memory and comparison are needed even to register that we have perceived.

5. Imagination and memory: decaying sense

Hobbes’s imagination is not a mysterious faculty. It is “decaying sense.” When the object is removed, the motion it caused does not cease instantly; it persists and gradually weakens. This continued internal motion is imagination. Memory and imagination are the same phenomenon considered differently: as mere image, it is imagination; as image recognized as past, it is memory.

This gives Hobbes a unified account of perception, memory, dreaming, and fiction. Simple imagination preserves a phantasm from sense. Compound imagination combines parts from previous sense, as when one imagines a centaur. In Human Nature, he explains dreams through motions from the inward parts of the body to the brain, especially reciprocal effects between the brain and vital parts. Memory is an internal sense of what has faded, not a separate immaterial archive.

De Corpore adds important details. Imagination is weakened sense in the absence of the object; in dreams, phantasms can appear as clear as waking sense because there is no competing external sensation. The passions and the heart can generate or shape dream images: heat can produce anger and the image of an enemy; cold can produce fear and ghostly apparitions. Hobbes therefore explains many visions and superstitious experiences through bodily causes, though he also leaves room for genuine supernatural communication where God acts specially.

This is one of Hobbes’s deepest contributions to philosophy of mind: perception, imagination, memory, hallucination, dream, and fiction differ by causal origin, intensity, order, and relation to present objects, not because they belong to different metaphysical substances.

6. Trains of thought: association, purpose, and foresight

Thought is not a static set of ideas. It is a train or succession of phantasms. Hobbes explains ordinary mental succession through the order in which sensory motions were originally connected. When we have experienced things together or in sequence, the later phantasm tends to follow the earlier.

There are also regulated trains of thought. A desire for an end can organize the sequence by calling forth images of means. The mind moves from a desired result to possible paths toward it. This is shared by human beings and other animals, because it depends on appetite, memory, and association, not yet on language.

Human foresight remains limited. Hobbes says the present exists in nature, the past exists in memory, and the future is only a fiction of the mind. Strictly divine foresight or providence belongs to God, “by whose will” things come to pass. Human beings can infer, expect, and calculate, but they do not naturally possess knowledge of the future as such.

This is significant for a philosophy of mind because Hobbes builds temporal cognition from memory, expectation, and causal inference rather than from a special faculty of rational intuition. Human future-directed thought is probabilistic, imaginative, and practical; divine providence is of another order.

7. Attention, study, wit, dullness, and madness

Hobbes also has a theory of attention. In De Corpore, attention or study occurs when one motion possesses the mind so strongly that it suppresses other sensory motions. Attention is not a pure act of a spiritual subject; it is a dominance relation among internal motions.

His account of mental difference is equally physiological and affective. In Leviathan, differences of wit arise from differences in passions, education, and bodily constitution. Thoughts are “scouts” and “spies” of desire, searching out paths toward desired objects. Weak passions make dullness; excessive or violently disordered passions produce madness.

This gives Hobbes a powerful anti-faculty psychology. Intelligence is not a separate rational essence implanted above the body. It is a pattern in the interaction of memory, appetite, speech, education, bodily constitution, and disciplined attention.

8. Language, names, and the making of reason

Human reason, for Hobbes, depends on speech. Animals have sense, imagination, memory, appetite, aversion, deliberation, and will. Human beings differ by their use of names as marks and signs. In Human Nature, Hobbes says humans create marks for memory and names as arbitrary signs of conceptions; this makes science possible in a way not available to beasts.

In Leviathan, the doctrine becomes more famous. Universals are not universal things. They are names. Nothing in the world is universal except names; things themselves are singular. Truth and falsity belong not to things but to speech. Definitions are therefore foundational for science, because errors in definitions propagate through reasoning.

Reason is computation. Hobbes defines it as adding and subtracting, or reckoning the consequences of general names. Understanding, in the properly human sense, is conception caused by speech. Children do not have reason before acquiring speech, and scientific reasoning requires well-ordered definitions and method.

This makes Hobbes both closer to and more distant from later computational theories of mind than is often noticed. He does define reason as reckoning; however, the Stanford Encyclopedia rightly cautions that the connection to modern artificial intelligence or computationalism is only rough. Hobbes’s “computation” is linguistic, nominalist, and embedded in a broader physiology of phantasm and appetite.

The philosophical result is precise:

Sense gives phantasms. Memory preserves decayed phantasms. Appetite gives direction. Names stabilize phantasms as marks. Speech turns private marks into public signs. Definitions discipline signs. Reason computes with signs. Science is the ordered knowledge of consequences.

9. Knowledge: sense, memory, evidence, and science

Hobbes distinguishes knowledge of fact from science. Knowledge of fact comes from sense and memory. Scientific knowledge is not bare memory of what happened, but evidence of the truth of propositions: knowledge of consequences from definitions and principles. Human Nature distinguishes sense/remembrance from science, and says evidence involves the concomitance of conceptions with words. Without evidence, syllogistic reasoning is parrot-like.

In Leviathan, Hobbes says there is no absolute discursive knowledge of past or future fact. Science is conditional: if definitions and premises are accepted, consequences follow. This is why scientific knowledge is more certain than ordinary prediction but also more dependent on proper naming.

The mind, then, is not primarily a contemplative faculty that intuits essences. It is a sense-based, memory-bearing, language-using calculating system. It knows facts by perception and memory; it knows consequences by names and definitions.

10. Passion, appetite, aversion, and conatus

Hobbes’s motive psychology begins with a distinction between vital and voluntary motion. Vital motions include blood circulation, pulse, breathing, digestion, and other motions that do not require imagination. Voluntary or animal motions include walking, speaking, and moving the limbs; these depend on a preceding thought or image. The first small internal beginning of voluntary motion is “endeavour.” When endeavour is toward something, it is appetite or desire; when away from something, it is aversion.

Pleasure and pain are motions around the heart that help or hinder vital motion. In De Corpore, Hobbes explicitly connects pleasure and pain to action on the heart and to vital motion, including Harvey’s circulation of the blood. Appetite and aversion are the first endeavours of animal motion, and experience is needed to learn what is pleasant or harmful.

This makes Hobbes’s affect theory thoroughly embodied. The passions are not irrational vapours opposed to a purely rational soul. They are internal motions continuous with the rest of the organism’s life. The Stanford Encyclopedia’s treatment of Hobbes on emotion emphasizes this continuity: Hobbes consistently treats passions as internal bodily motions tied to vital and voluntary motion, imagination, deliberation, will, the brain, the heart, and the circulation of the blood.

Good and evil are also indexed to appetite and aversion. What a person calls good is generally what he desires; what he calls evil is what he avoids. Hobbes denies an absolute common rule of good and evil outside a commonwealth or other authoritative arbiter.

This does not mean Hobbes has no normativity. It means natural evaluative language begins in appetite and aversion, while stable public norms require law, covenant, and civil authority. At the psychological level, value is conative before it is juridical.

11. Deliberation and will

Deliberation is not a special rational faculty floating above desire. It is the alternating sequence of appetites, aversions, hopes, fears, and other passions concerning what one may do. Will is the last appetite or aversion immediately preceding action. It is not a separate faculty. Hobbes explicitly says beasts deliberate and have will.

This is one of Hobbes’s clearest anti-scholastic moves. He does not treat the will as a metaphysical power of free election independent of causal motion. Will is an event in the sequence of appetitive motions. De Corpore states the same view: deliberation is a series of appetites and aversions, and the will is the last act after deliberation; there is no freedom from necessity in the will.

The best deliberator is not the person with an uncaused will. It is the person with the greatest and surest prospect of consequences, acquired through experience and reason. Hobbes also denies the possibility of final tranquillity in this life: life itself is motion, and cannot be without desire, fear, and sense.

This is a compatibilist and necessitarian philosophy of agency. Actions can be voluntary because they proceed from appetite, aversion, deliberation, and will; they need not be uncaused. Indeed, for Hobbes, an uncaused act would be unintelligible.

12. Self-knowledge, conscience, and inward reading

Hobbes famously tells the reader to “read thyself.” But this is not Cartesian introspection into an immaterial ego. It is a disciplined observation of passions, thoughts, hopes, fears, and motives in oneself as a guide to understanding others. Hobbes immediately cautions that while the passions are broadly the same in all, their objects differ by constitution and education; only God, “he that searcheth hearts,” fully reads the heart.

Conscience is also demystified. Hobbes says “conscience” originally meant shared knowledge, but later came to mean a person’s private opinion or judgment. Judgment, opinion, and doubt are treated analogously to will and deliberation.

So Hobbes does not deny inwardness. He denies that inwardness reveals a metaphysically separate soul. Introspection is real, but partial; self-knowledge is useful, but not infallible; conscience is psychologically and socially powerful, but not a private oracle of immaterial reason.

13. The soul: life, living body, and mortalism

Hobbes’s doctrine of the soul is one of the most delicate parts of his philosophy of mind. He does not simply deny Christian hope. He denies that the soul is naturally and philosophically demonstrable as a separable incorporeal substance.

In Leviathan XXXVIII, Hobbes argues that Scripture does not clearly teach the natural immortality of a soul independent of the body. Eternal life belongs to resurrection, judgment, promise, and divine grace, not to a naturally immortal mental substance that survives death on its own.

In Leviathan XLIV, he is even more explicit. He treats the doctrine of natural immortality of every soul as false in his reading of Scripture. “Soul” often means life or living creature; body and soul means body alive. If “soul” meant a separated incorporeal substance, Hobbes says the same problem would apply to animals. When Scripture speaks of body and soul cast into hell, he reads this as body and life, that is, being cast alive.

He also argues that the dead do not naturally know anything before resurrection. The patriarchs are “alive” by divine promise, not because their separated souls are naturally active in the interim.

French scholarship on “Christian mortalism” usefully clarifies the point. Lessay’s study presents Hobbes’s position as a mortalist doctrine reconstructed especially from Leviathan XXXVIII, XLII, XLIV, XLV, XLVII, and the Latin Appendix: “soul” means life or living creature, not an incorporeal substance derived from Aristotle and scholasticism; having a soul and being alive are effectively the same.

Spanish-language scholarship adds another nuance: Hobbes’s mortalism should not be treated as a simple automatic deduction from materialism. Work from the Hobbesiana Argentina context stresses that earlier Hobbes texts such as The Elements and De Cive still maintain immortality in a more traditional theological register, while Leviathan and later writings move toward explicit denial of natural separated immortality. That scholarship also emphasizes the political use of mortalism: by attacking clerical control over fears of postmortem punishment, Hobbes weakens a rival source of authority.

The careful conclusion is this: Hobbes’s “soul” is not a Cartesian mind-substance. In ordinary human beings, the soul is life, or the living body considered as alive. Natural death interrupts natural mental activity. Future blessed life depends on God’s power, promise, and resurrection, not on the inherent immortality of an incorporeal soul.

14. Spirit: breath, subtle body, disposition, apparition, and divine power

Hobbes’s treatment of “spirit” is philological, metaphysical, and theological at once. In Leviathan XXXIV, he begins from the many meanings of the biblical and ordinary words translated as spirit: wind, breath, subtle body, ghost, apparition, disposition, inclination, passion, disease, ability, and divine power.

This is not mere wordplay. Hobbes thinks metaphysics goes wrong when words are detached from coherent signification. A “spirit,” if it is a substance, must have dimensions and place, even if it is subtle, invisible, fluid, or imperceptible. An apparition in a dream or vision may be a phantasm in the brain. A metaphorical spirit may be a disposition or power. The Spirit of God may mean God himself, or God’s power, or divine gifts, depending on the scriptural context.

French scholarship on Hobbes and the body of God emphasizes that in The Elements Hobbes already defines spirit as a natural body subtle enough to escape the senses, and that conceiving spirit requires dimension; “substance without dimension” is contradictory.

This gives Hobbes a taxonomy useful for philosophy of mind:

A spirit may be a subtle body.
A spirit may be an apparition or phantasm.
A spirit may be a disposition, temper, or passion.
A spirit may be divine power.
But a spirit cannot, strictly speaking, be a no-place incorporeal substance.

This is central to Hobbes’s philosophy of mind because it controls how one may speak about ghosts, angels, divine inspiration, the Holy Spirit, and the soul without abandoning the ontology of body and place.

15. Angels: not dismissed, but relocated

Hobbes’s account of angels is often flattened into “he rejected angels.” That is too crude. The actual position is more subtle.

In Leviathan XXXIV, Hobbes says “angel” means messenger. In many Old Testament contexts, angels can be understood as God’s extraordinary presence, power, or message appearing in dreams, visions, clouds, fire, or other forms. Such appearances may be supernatural images in fancy, sent by God as messages.

Yet Hobbes does not simply deny angelic reality. He says the New Testament gives stronger reason to believe in substantial and permanent angels. Christ’s language and other New Testament passages compel belief in angels as real beings, but not as incorporeal no-place substances. If angels are substances, they must be bodies with dimensions and place, however subtle.

This is a crucial point for extracting a philosophy of mind. Hobbes allows two main models for angelic mentality or angelic appearance.

On the first model, some “angels” are supernatural phantasms or divinely caused appearances. In that case, the immediate mental phenomenon is within human imagination or vision, but the message is divine. The angelic event belongs to the interface between God’s power and human phantasm.

On the second model, angels are real subtle bodies. In that case, they are not immaterial minds; they are located substances, and any mentality they possess must be compatible with corporeal existence. Hobbes gives no developed angelic psychology, but his ontology requires that angelic minds, if they are minds of substances, be minds of bodies.

This makes angels philosophically important. They are not decorative theology. They force Hobbes to clarify whether “mind” requires ordinary animal organs, whether supernatural agency can communicate through phantasms, and whether imperceptible bodies can be genuine agents.

16. Inspiration and the Holy Spirit

Hobbes’s treatment of inspiration follows the same disciplined semantics. To say that prophets speak “by the Spirit of God” does not mean, for Hobbes, that an incorporeal ghost-substance enters them. It may mean that God speaks through supernatural dream, vision, voice, or gift. “Full of the Holy Spirit” can mean endowed with divine gifts or powers, not filled with a literal portion of divine substance.

Again, the point is not reductionist dismissal. Hobbes is not saying prophecy is always fraud or delusion. He is saying that if prophecy is genuine, its mechanism must not be described through incoherent metaphysics. God may act through dreams, visions, voices, and extraordinary phantasms; but no explanatory gain is achieved by inventing an incorporeal substance without place or dimension.

For Hobbes’s philosophy of mind, inspiration becomes a supernatural modification of imagination, audition, or vision under divine agency. It is not a separate immaterial faculty.

17. The mind of God: incomprehensibility, honour, providence, and late corporeity

The mind of God is the most sensitive boundary case in Hobbes’s philosophy of mind.

In Leviathan III, Hobbes says that whatever we imagine is finite. We have no image or conception of infinity. The name of God is not used to conceive God — whose nature and power are incomprehensible — but to honour him.

In Leviathan XXXIV, he repeats the point: the nature of God is incomprehensible; divine attributes are not literal descriptions of God’s essence but expressions of honour. The “Spirit of God” can mean God himself, but God exceeds human understanding.

This apophatic strain is essential. Hobbes’s ordinary theory of mind begins with sense, phantasm, name, and reckoning. But God is not an object of sensory phantasm, and therefore not an object of ordinary conception. Human beings can reason to God as first cause and sovereign power, and can receive revelation, but they cannot form a finite mental image of God’s essence. Spanish-language work on Hobbes’s theology captures this well: God’s existence and providence remain objective for Hobbes, but divine will is analogical and not directly conceivable; it is manifested to humans through reason, revelation, and the prophet.

There is, however, a later development. John Henry’s 2024 study argues that Hobbes’s early position is largely apophatic: God is known to exist, but not known as to what he is. By 1668, especially in the Latin Leviathan and related controversy, Hobbes speaks more explicitly of God as a corporeal spirit. Henry argues that this late doctrine should be taken seriously, not dismissed as a mask for atheism, while also noting possible influence from Henry More, Stoicism, or aether theories.

French and Spanish-language discussions of this issue sharpen the tension. French work on Hobbes’s “body of God” notes Hobbes’s late claim that God is a spirit but corporeal, and that Scripture calls spirits subtle or invisible but never literally incorporeal in the strict metaphysical sense. Spanish scholarship on “God according to Hobbes” emphasizes the difficult idea of God as an “infinitely fine Spirit,” where spirit means a thin, fluid, transparent, invisible body, while also pressing the philosophical tension between corporeality, infinity, causation, and Hobbes’s usual account of bodies as extended and finite.

The best reconstruction is developmental and layered.

Early and middle Hobbes: God is real, sovereign, causal, providential, and incomprehensible. Human beings have no sensory image or positive idea of divine essence. Divine mental predicates such as will, knowledge, wisdom, and foresight are used under the discipline of honour, analogy, and Scripture.

Late Hobbes: Hobbes increasingly applies his ontology of body and place even to God, speaking of God as a corporeal spirit. This preserves monism — nothing real is a no-place immaterial substance — but creates deep philosophical tensions about infinity, divine simplicity, and the relation between God’s body and the created world.

For philosophy of mind, the mind of God cannot be treated as merely a larger human mind. It is either incomprehensible under the apophatic doctrine or, in late Hobbes, the agency of an infinitely subtle corporeal spirit whose operations remain beyond ordinary human cognition. Either way, God’s mind marks the limit of Hobbes’s naturalized psychology, not an optional addendum.

18. Animals, children, and the gradation of minds

Hobbes’s philosophy of mind is not anthropocentric in the scholastic sense. Animals have sense, imagination, memory, appetite, aversion, deliberation, and will. Hobbes explicitly says beasts deliberate and have will.

What animals lack is not all mentality but speech-based reason and science. Children before speech likewise lack reason in the strict Hobbesian sense, because reason is reckoning with names.

This yields a graded theory of mind.

All suitably organized animals may have sense and phantasm.
Many animals have memory, imagination, appetite, aversion, deliberation, and will.
Humans have all of these plus speech.
Speech gives names.
Names give reckoning.
Reckoning gives science.
Civil life gives public normativity and authoritative definitions.

This is a major anti-Cartesian result. Hobbes does not reserve mentality for human rational souls. He reserves linguistic science for humans, but not sensation, desire, or will.

19. Religion, fear, curiosity, and true worship

Hobbes gives a natural psychology of religion, but he does not simply reduce all religion to error. In Leviathan VI, he treats curiosity as the desire to know why and how, and describes religion in connection with fear of invisible power. Such invisible power may be feigned, socially transmitted, or true; if the power is truly such as imagined, the result is true religion.

French work on Hobbes’s religion stresses that his religious thought is systematic rather than a removable ornament. It involves both an anthropological account of religion through passions and a theological determination through divine omnipotence and natural law.

This matters for philosophy of mind because religious cognition in Hobbes involves fear, curiosity, causal inference, imagination, language, political authority, and revelation. A Hobbesian philosophy of mind cannot simply bracket theology; religion is one of the most important test cases for how human beings form beliefs about invisible causes.

20. Hobbes against scholastic faculty psychology

Hobbes uses words such as “faculty,” especially in earlier texts, but his explanatory tendency is anti-faculty. He does not want to explain memory by positing a faculty of memory, or will by a faculty of will, or reason by a rational soul. He wants to identify the motions, signs, organs, and causal sequences that produce the phenomena.

Spanish work on Hobbes and psychology summarizes this as the end of the traditional psychology of faculties of the soul: Hobbes redefines the human being through materialist and physicalist principles, passions, and instrumental reason.

The critique is especially sharp in his handling of absurd phrases. “Immaterial substance,” “free will” when understood as a faculty independent of causal necessity, and similar expressions become examples of words that lack coherent signification. Hobbes’s cure is definitional discipline: begin with clear names tied to possible conceptions, and do not allow grammar to manufacture impossible entities.

21. Hobbes against Descartes

The contrast with Descartes is fundamental. Descartes argues from thinking to a thinking substance whose essence is thought. Hobbes replies, in effect, that from “I think” one may infer that something thinks, but not that this something is an immaterial substance. The subject of thought may be a body. Cairn’s study of Hobbes, Descartes, and More emphasizes Hobbes’s early argument that reasoning depends on names, names on imagination, and imagination and sense on motions in bodily organs; mind is thus located in the motions of an organic body.

French work on Hobbes in relation to Port-Royal similarly notes that Hobbes contests the Cartesian move from thought to a substance whose essence is thought, and links that critique to his broader rejection of scholastic faculty language.

The key dispute is not whether thinking exists. Hobbes does not deny thought. The dispute is what kind of thing the thinker is. Descartes says an immaterial thinking substance. Hobbes says a living body whose internal motions produce phantasms, names, passions, and reckoning.

22. Reconstructed Hobbesian philosophy of mind

A comprehensive Hobbesian philosophy of mind can be reconstructed as follows.

First, the metaphysical principle: all substances are bodies, and all bodily change is motion. A mind, if it exists naturally, must be the mind of a body.

Second, the biological principle: life is organized vital motion. The heart, blood, nerves, brain, organs, and “spirits” are not incidental; they are the physiological substrate of mental life.

Third, the sensory principle: all finite human conception begins in sense. There are no innate intelligible species or purely intellectual images of immaterial essences.

Fourth, the phenomenal principle: sensible qualities are phantasms or appearances in the sentient creature, caused by external bodies but not literally existing in them as they appear.

Fifth, the mnemonic principle: imagination is decaying sense, and memory is imagination considered as past.

Sixth, the associative principle: trains of thought follow earlier sensory conjunctions or are directed by appetitive ends.

Seventh, the linguistic principle: names are marks for memory and signs for communication. Universals are names, not universal beings.

Eighth, the rational principle: reason is computation with names. Science is knowledge of consequences from definitions, not direct intuition of essences.

Ninth, the affective principle: passions are internal motions, especially endeavours toward or away from objects as they help or hinder vital motion.

Tenth, the volitional principle: deliberation is alternating appetite and aversion; will is the last appetite or aversion before action.

Eleventh, the evaluative principle: good and evil begin as names for what is desired or avoided, while stable public standards require authority.

Twelfth, the personal principle: the self is not an immaterial ego but the living, remembering, speaking, desiring, socially situated body.

Thirteenth, the mortalist principle: the human soul is life or living body, not a naturally separable incorporeal substance. Resurrection and eternal life depend on divine power and promise.

Fourteenth, the angelological principle: angels, if substantial, are subtle bodies; if apparitional, they are divinely caused phantasms or messages. They are not no-place incorporeal substances.

Fifteenth, the theological principle: God is real and sovereign, but human beings cannot form a finite conception of his essence. In Hobbes’s later writings, God is increasingly described as a corporeal spirit, but divine mind remains a limit case rather than a human-style psychology.

23. Modern classifications: what Hobbes is and is not

Hobbes is a materialist, but not a modern neural identity theorist. He lacks modern neurophysiology and works with seventeenth-century mechanics, optics, circulation theory, and animal spirits.

He is a physicalist in spirit, but not a reductive eliminativist in the simple sense. He does not abolish talk of sense, imagination, appetite, deliberation, or will; he redefines them as bodily motions.

He is not a behaviorist. Inner events matter greatly for him.

He is not a panpsychist. Sense belongs to living creatures with the relevant organization, not to all bodies indiscriminately.

He is not a Cartesian dualist. Thought does not prove an immaterial thinking substance.

He is not merely a proto-computationalist. Reason is reckoning, but it is reckoning with names within an embodied, passionate, political, and linguistic animal.

He is a compatibilist or necessitarian about will. Voluntary action is action from appetite, aversion, and deliberation, not action outside causation.

He is a Christian mortalist in his mature scriptural psychology of the soul. This does not mean he denies resurrection; it means he denies natural separated immortality.

24. Major tensions and unresolved problems

Several tensions remain.

The first is the relation between appearance and object. Hobbes says sensory qualities are phantasms in us, caused by external motion. But he must still explain why phantasms represent external objects at all. His outward-reaction theory gives one answer, but it remains underdeveloped by later standards.

The second is the relation between physiology and semantics. Sense and appetite can be explained by motion, but reason depends on names, definitions, and public rules. Hobbes therefore needs both mechanical physiology and linguistic convention.

The third is the status of God. Early Hobbes emphasizes divine incomprehensibility; later Hobbes speaks of God as corporeal spirit. Scholars disagree about whether this is development, clarification, strategic provocation, Stoic influence, response to Henry More, or a deeper feature of Hobbes’s monism. Henry argues that the corporeal God should be taken seriously, while Spanish-language scholarship highlights the internal philosophical strains of an infinite corporeal deity within Hobbes’s framework.

The fourth is angelic mentality. Hobbes gives no full psychology of angels. He gives a metaphysical and scriptural rule: they are either apparitional messages or subtle bodies. That is enough to exclude immaterial angelic minds, but not enough to explain their cognition in detail.

The fifth is the early-to-late development on immortality. Spanish Hobbes scholarship is right to caution that Hobbes’s mortalism is not simply present in the same form from the beginning. The early works can preserve traditional immortality more readily, while Leviathan and later writings make the mortalist consequences more explicit.

The sixth is political motivation. Hobbes’s reinterpretation of soul, spirit, hell, demons, and clergy is politically charged. But political usefulness does not make the doctrine insincere. In Hobbes, scriptural exegesis, metaphysics, and sovereign authority are intertwined.

25. The non-English reception: what it adds

French scholarship is especially useful on three points. First, it emphasizes that accusations of materialism and atheism in Hobbes’s reception were not accidental; Bernhardt notes that the very terms “materialist” and “materialism” in seventeenth- and eighteenth-century English contexts appear deeply provoked by Hobbes, especially for the Cambridge Platonists. Second, French work on Port-Royal clarifies how Hobbes’s semantic critique of “incorporeal substance” undermines both Cartesian and scholastic accounts of mind. Third, French work on Christian mortalism clarifies that Hobbes’s denial of natural soul-immortality is a theological doctrine about resurrection and life, not merely a secular negation.

Spanish and Latin American scholarship adds a different emphasis. The Costa Rican discussion of God in Hobbes highlights both the seriousness of Hobbes’s divine sovereignty and the tensions in calling God an infinitely subtle corporeal spirit. The Argentine Hobbesiana work on immortality stresses development across Hobbes’s corpus and warns against deriving mortalism mechanically from materialism alone; it also draws attention to the political function of undermining clerical control over eschatological fear. Spanish work on Hobbes and psychology emphasizes Hobbes’s dismantling of traditional faculty psychology and the replacement of rational-soul anthropology with a passion-centered materialist psychology.

Taken together, these non-English materials make the theological Hobbes harder to ignore. They resist the overly tidy Anglo-American habit of treating God, angels, demons, and soul as embarrassing appendices to an otherwise secular political theory. In Hobbes, those topics are central tests of the entire system.

26. Final synthesis

Hobbes’s philosophy of mind can be stated in one sentence: a mind is the ordered activity of a living body moved by external bodies, retaining their motions as phantasms, directed by appetite and aversion, stabilized by names, calculating by speech, and governed socially by law.

But that sentence must be expanded for soul, God, and angels.

The human soul is not a separable immaterial substance. It is life, or the living body considered as alive. Death naturally ends mental activity; resurrection is a divine act, not the automatic survival of a ghostly mind.

Angels are not casually dismissed. Hobbes treats them through Scripture and semantics. Some angelic appearances are divine messages in dreams or visions; substantial angels, especially under New Testament pressure, must be subtle bodies with place and dimensions. Angelic mind, if substantial, is corporeal mind.

God is not a psychological object. Human beings cannot form a finite conception of the divine essence. Divine predicates are governed by honour, analogy, revelation, and sovereignty. Yet Hobbes’s late corporeal language about God shows the radical reach of his monism: if God is called a substance or spirit in strict metaphysical terms, Hobbes increasingly wants even that to be compatible with body and place. This doctrine is philosophically tense, but it is not trivial or merely decorative.

The result is one of the most ambitious early modern alternatives to Cartesian dualism. Hobbes gives a unified account of sensation, imagination, memory, dream, emotion, will, reason, language, madness, religion, soul, angels, and God under the pressure of a single demand: do not posit entities that cannot be located, named coherently, caused intelligibly, or warranted by disciplined scriptural interpretation.

PreviousFrancisco Suárez NextRené Descartes

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
